title: Proverbs 23
subtitle: Thirty Sayings: Counsel on Wealth, Parents, Wine; Wisdom's Future Hope

sections.header: Verses 1–18 — Thirty Sayings: Don't Crave; Move Not the Boundary; Discipline Your Heart

heb.paragraph: Do not crave the ruler's maṭʿammôt — his delicacies — for that food is deceptive (v. 3). The word implies carefully prepared, appetising food designed to impress. The sage warns that dining with the powerful is transactional: the delicacies come with strings attached. Social climbing through the stomach is a trap.

Do not move (ʾal-tassēg) an ancient boundary stone or encroach on the fields of the fatherless (v. 10). The orphan theme intensifies the warning from 22:28: those without a father to defend their inheritance are the most vulnerable to land theft. Their gōʾēl (redeemer/kinsman defender) is God himself — the strongest possible deterrent.

refs.note: Do not move your neighbour's boundary stone — the legal basis for v.10's boundary-stone warning.
Do not move an ancient boundary stone — the direct parallel, establishing the principle as a repeated warning.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The eagle-wings image — wealth does not merely decrease but sprouts wings and flies. The personification captures the experience of sudden loss more vividly than any abstract statement about impermanence.
Do not speak to a fool — Alter notes the instruction acknowledges the limits of wisdom's reach. Not every person is reachable; the fool who scorns wisdom cannot be helped by more wisdom.

waltke.source: Bruce K. Waltke, NICOT Commentary on Proverbs (2 vols.) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Do not wear yourself out to get rich — the eagle-wings image for vanishing wealth. The counsel is against the exhausting pursuit of wealth as the supreme good, not against diligence itself.
Do not move the ancient boundary stone — the fatherless have a strong Defender (go'el): the LORD himself. Moving the boundary is a legal offence against the most powerful possible advocate.
Calvin: 'Do not wear yourself out to get rich — this is a direct command against avarice. The exhausted pursuit of wealth is both spiritually and physically destructive.'
Calvin: 'The fatherless have a strong Defender — those who steal from the powerless make God their adversary. The absence of a human advocate does not leave the fatherless unprotected.'

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on the eagle-wings image for vanishing wealth traces the bird metaphor through the OT: wealth that sprouts wings and flies away like an eagle toward heaven is an image of sudden, complete loss. The note identifies this as one of Proverbs' most vivid wealth-impermanence images — not gradual decline but sudden, irreversible departure.

NET: go'el (Defender) — the kinsman-redeemer who advocates for the vulnerable. God as go'el of the fatherless makes encroachment on their land a legal offence against the most powerful possible advocate.

hist.context: The first half of ch.23 continues the Thirty Sayings with warnings against craving the ruler's food (v.3), exploiting the poor (v.10), and neglecting discipline (vv.13-14). The boundary-stone warning (v.10) is among the most pointed social ethics in the collection.

ane.similarity: Both collections feature a court sage dispensing wisdom through short sayings, fables, and numerical proverbs. Both warn against loose speech, praise discipline of children, and use animal imagery for moral instruction. Ahiqar's setting in the Assyrian court parallels Proverbs' attribution to Solomon's court.
ane.difference: Ahiqar's frame narrative — a betrayed courtier vindicated by his wisdom — adds a dramatic element absent from Proverbs. The Aramaic text includes fables and riddles that Proverbs largely omits. Proverbs maintains a more consistently theological framework.
ane.significance: Ahiqar demonstrates that wisdom literature circulated across linguistic and cultural boundaries in the ANE. The Aramaic text was known from Egypt to Elephantine, suggesting an international wisdom network in which Israelite sages participated.

sections.header: Verses 19–35 — Listen to Parents; Wisdom's Future Hope; The Warning Against Wine

heb.paragraph: There is surely an ʾaḥărît — a future, an outcome — for you, and your hope will not be cut off (v. 18). This is one of Proverbs' rare eschatological hints: the righteous have a future that extends beyond present suffering. The word means 'afterness' — what comes at the end. It grounds present discipline in future vindication.

The extended warning against wine (vv. 29–35) is Proverbs' most vivid alcohol passage. Yayin is standard grape wine; its effects are described with clinical precision — red eyes, bruises, hallucinations ('your eyes will see strange things'), loss of pain sensation ('they struck me but I did not feel it'). The final line captures addiction's cycle: 'When will I wake up? I will seek another drink.'

refs.note: It is not for kings to drink wine — the royal wine-warning that mirrors ch.23's extended treatment.
Do not get drunk on wine, which leads to debauchery — the NT application of the wine-warning principle.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Buy wisdom and never sell it — Alter notes placing wisdom in the economic register establishes it as the currency against which all other exchanges are measured.
The drunkard who wakes seeking another drink — Alter notes this is one of the Bible's most precise descriptions of the addiction cycle. The final verse is both comic and devastating.

waltke.source: Bruce K. Waltke, NICOT Commentary on Proverbs (2 vols.) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Buy truth, wisdom, instruction, understanding and do not sell them — Waltke notes wisdom placed in the economic register of irreplaceable assets. Acquire at cost; never trade away.
The extended wine poem — Proverbs' most vivid and psychologically precise anti-alcohol passage, describing the full addiction cycle including the drunkard who wakes seeking another drink.
Calvin: 'Buy truth and do not sell it — once you have genuine wisdom, it must never be traded away for temporal advantage. It is the asset that all other assets depend on.'

Calvin: 'The nature of all sensual addiction — the pleasure is brief, the pain enduring, and the first desire upon recovery is to return to the source of the pain. Solomon presents this with the irony that makes it recognisable.'

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on qanah (buy/acquire) traces the root through Proverbs' consistent use for acquiring wisdom (4:5, 7; 16:16; 23:23) and through Gen 4:1 ('I have acquired a man with the LORD's help'). The note discusses how the commercial metaphor establishes wisdom as costly to acquire and worth any price — once genuinely possessed, it must never be traded away.

The NET note on 'do not gaze at wine when it is red, when it sparkles in the cup, when it goes down smoothly' explains that the warning is at the moment of temptation, not merely at the point of intoxication. Wisdom addresses the decision before it is made. The sensory appeal of wine — its colour, sparkle, and smooth taste — is precisely what must not be allowed to govern the choice.

hist.context: The second half addresses the parental heart's joy when children are wise (vv.19-25), the warning against the adulterous woman (vv.26-28), and the chapter's most extended passage: the warning against wine (vv.29-35). The wine passage is the most vivid description of addiction in the OT.

ppl.name: The righteous
ppl.text: Integrity is both compass and shield.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIV"Surely there is a future hope for you, and your hope will not be cut off."NoteAmong the most encouraging verses in Proverbs. Wisdom's discipline has a guaranteed future. The path does not close; it opens toward a secure hope.

src.title: Ancient Near Eastern wisdom
src.quote: These proverbs reflect Israel's engagement with international wisdom traditions, especially Egyptian.
src.note: Wisdom was a shared human enterprise across cultures.

src.title: Hezekian editorial work
src.quote: Proverbs 25:1 notes that men of Hezekiah copied these proverbs — one of the most historically specific attributions in Proverbs.
src.note: The editorial activity shows Proverbs as a living, curated collection.

rec.text: Proverbs 21-30 has been foundational to Christian virtue ethics — the practical outworking of the fear of the LORD.
These chapters have been widely cited in ethics, governance, and social teaching.

rows.label: Buy Wisdom; Never Sell It
rows.text: Buy the truth and do not sell it -- wisdom, instruction, and insight as well. Wisdom placed in the economic register of irreplaceable assets. Acquire it at cost; never trade it away. Once genuinely possessed, it must never be sold for temporal advantage.

rows.label: The Wine Poem: Addiction Described
rows.text: Who has woe? Who has sorrow? Those who linger over wine. The extended wine poem is the Bible's most precise description of the addiction cycle -- from the seductive colours and smoothness to the drunkard who wakes seeking another drink.

tx.title: Wine passage — vivid Hebrew preserved
tx.content: MT's extended wine-warning passage uses vivid, almost poetic language ('Who has bloodshot eyes? ... In the end it bites like a snake'). The LXX translates with comparable vividness but occasionally reorders the images. Targum Proverbs follows MT's order but expands the moral lesson.
tx.note: This passage's literary quality made it memorable and resistant to textual alteration. Its preservation across versions shows how poetic power can stabilize transmission.

positions.name: Physical discipline endorsed within appropriate limits
positions.argument: The rod-passages form a consistent pattern. Applied wisely and in love, corporal discipline is a legitimate parental tool.

positions.name: The principle universal; the method contextualised
positions.argument: The principle — firm discipline is an act of love — is non-negotiable. The specific method requires contextualisation to contemporary understanding.

prayer_prompt: Apply your heart to instruction and your ears to words of knowledge. Consider asking God about your appetite for wisdom — are you still hungry for instruction?

coaching.tip: 'Do not wear yourself out to get rich; do not trust your own cleverness' (v. 4). The Thirty Sayings warn against obsessive wealth pursuit. Riches 'sprout wings and fly off to the sky like an eagle' (v. 5). The imagery is vivid — you grasp and grasp, and wealth flies away. Moderation isn't laziness; it's realism.

'Listen to your father, who gave you life, and do not despise your mother when she is old' (v. 22). Parental honor persists into their old age. This isn't just about obedient children but about adult children caring for aging parents. Wisdom extends through the whole lifespan of the family relationship.
